verses.text: And it came to pass, when king Hezekiah heard it, that he rent his clothes, and covered himself with sackcloth, and went into the house of the LORD. And he sent Eliakim, which was over the household, and Shebna the scribe, and the elders of the priests, covered with sackcloth, to Isaiah the prophet the son of Amoz. And they said unto him, Thus saith Hezekiah, This day is a day of trouble, and of rebuke, and blasphemy: for the children are come to the birth, and there is not strength to bring forth. It may be the LORD thy God will hear all the words of Rabshakeh, whom the king of Assyria his master hath sent to reproach the living God; and will reprove the words which the LORD thy God hath heard: wherefore lift up thy prayer for the remnant that are left. Guidance: Hezekiah turns to the LORD and the prophet. The king's response to the Rabshakeh's threats is the model of faith under siege. He tears his clothes, puts on sackcloth (the garb of mourning and humble repentance), and 'went into the house of the LORD' — he takes the crisis straight to God's presence. Then he sends his officials, also in sackcloth, to Isaiah the prophet. His message is rich with honest faith. He names the day truthfully: 'a day of trouble, and of rebuke, and blasphemy.' He uses a vivid image of helplessness: 'the children are come to the birth, and there is not strength to bring forth' — Judah is like a woman in labor who has no strength to deliver; the crisis has reached its peak and human strength has failed. And then the hinge of hope: 'It may be the LORD thy God will hear all the words of Rabshakeh... to reproach the living God.' Hezekiah seizes on the Rabshakeh's blasphemy not as Judah's problem but as the LORD's — the Assyrian has insulted 'the living God,' and Hezekiah appeals to God to defend His own name. He asks Isaiah to 'lift up thy prayer for the remnant.' The chapter is showing us the right response to overwhelming threat: go to the LORD's house, humble yourself, seek the prophet's intercession, and reframe the battle as God's own honor at stake. The chapter is teaching us the contrast with chapter 18's faltering start: there Hezekiah tried to pay tribute; here, when that has failed and the threat is direct blasphemy against God, he goes to the temple and to prayer. The chapter is teaching us that the phrase 'the living God' is the key — the Rabshakeh treated the LORD as a dead idol like the gods of Hamath; Hezekiah knows He is THE LIVING God, and that an insult to the living God will not go unanswered. The chapter is teaching us to bring our deepest crises into God's presence, to be honest about our helplessness ('no strength to bring forth'), and to make God's honor, not merely our survival, the ground of our appeal.

So the servants of king Hezekiah came to Isaiah. And Isaiah said unto them, Thus shall ye say to your master, Thus saith the LORD, Be not afraid of the words which thou hast heard, with which the servants of the king of Assyria have blasphemed me. Behold, I will send a blast upon him, and he shall hear a rumour, and shall return to his own land; and I will cause him to fall by the sword in his own land. Guidance: Isaiah's first answer — fear not. The LORD's reply through Isaiah is swift and reassuring: 'Be not afraid of the words which thou hast heard, with which the servants of the king of Assyria have blasphemed me.' Note the LORD's framing: He calls the Rabshakeh's words blasphemy against ME — He has taken the insult personally, exactly as Hezekiah hoped (v4). The promise is specific: 'I will send a blast upon him [a spirit or impulse], and he shall hear a rumour, and shall return to his own land; and I will cause him to fall by the sword in his own land.' Every clause of this brief prophecy will be fulfilled precisely in the rest of the chapter: Sennacherib will hear a report (of Tirhakah, v9), will return to his own land (v36), and will fall by the sword in his own land (v37, assassinated in Nineveh). The chapter is showing us the LORD answering His trusting king before the deliverance is visible. The threat is undiminished — the army still surrounds Judah — but the word has come: do not be afraid. The chapter is teaching us that faith rests on God's word before it sees God's act. Hezekiah will have to hold this promise through more threats (the second letter, v9-13) before it is fulfilled; the deliverance is certain in the word long before it appears in fact. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's 'Be not afraid' is not a denial of the danger but the announcement of a greater reality: the One who has been blasphemed will act, and the proud blasphemer will fall. The chapter is teaching us, in the words 'with which... they have blasphemed ME,' the deep comfort that when God's people are reproached for trusting Him, the reproach lands on God Himself — and He will not leave His own honor undefended.

So Rabshakeh returned, and found the king of Assyria warring against Libnah: for he had heard that he was departed from Lachish. And when he heard say of Tirhakah king of Ethiopia, Behold, he is come out to fight against thee: he sent messengers again unto Hezekiah, saying, Thus shall ye speak to Hezekiah king of Judah, saying, Let not thy God in whom thou trustest deceive thee, saying, Jerusalem shall not be delivered into the hand of the king of Assyria. Behold, thou hast heard what the kings of Assyria have done to all lands, by destroying them utterly: and shalt thou be delivered? Have the gods of the nations delivered them which my fathers have destroyed; as Gozan, and Haran, and Rezeph, and the children of Eden which were in Thelasar? Where is the king of Hamath, and the king of Arpad, and the king of the city of Sepharvaim, of Hena, and Ivah? Guidance: The second, written threat — blasphemy escalates. The LORD's prophesied 'rumour' arrives: Sennacherib hears that Tirhakah, the Egyptian/Cushite pharaoh, is marching against him, and must turn to deal with this new threat. But before withdrawing, he sends Hezekiah a second message — this time a letter — to keep the psychological pressure on. The content escalates the blasphemy. The first message (ch 18) had Hezekiah's God among the gods that could not deliver; this one directly attacks Hezekiah's trust: 'Let not thy God in whom thou trustest deceive thee.' He again lists conquered cities and their helpless gods — Gozan, Haran, Rezeph, Eden/Telassar, Hamath, Arpad, Sepharvaim, Hena, Ivah — and asks, in effect, what makes Judah's God any different. The chapter is showing us the relentlessness of the assault on faith: even as the LORD's deliverance begins to unfold (Tirhakah's threat forcing a withdrawal), the enemy doubles down with a written campaign of intimidation. The chapter is teaching us that the pressure on our trust often intensifies precisely when deliverance is near — the last assault is frequently the fiercest. The chapter is also teaching us the persistent theological error of the proud: Sennacherib genuinely cannot conceive that the God of little Judah is categorically different from the dead idols of Hamath and Arpad. His whole worldview is that gods are local, tribal, and as conquerable as the nations they represent. The chapter is teaching us that this is the fatal blindness of human pride — to measure the living God by the same yardstick as the works of men's hands, never imagining that the Maker of heaven and earth (as Hezekiah's prayer will declare) is in a different category altogether. The written threat, like the spoken one, will become the very document Hezekiah spreads before the LORD — the enemy's escalating blasphemy only deepens the ground of his appeal.

But before withdrawing, he sends Hezekiah a second message — this time a letter — to keep the psychological pressure on. The content escalates the blasphemy. The first message (ch 18) had Hezekiah's God among the gods that could not deliver; this one directly attacks Hezekiah's trust: 'Let not thy God in whom thou trustest deceive thee.' He again lists conquered cities and their helpless gods — Gozan, Haran, Rezeph, Eden/Telassar, Hamath, Arpad, Sepharvaim, Hena, Ivah — and asks, in effect, what makes Judah's God any different. The chapter is showing us the relentlessness of the assault on faith: even as the LORD's deliverance begins to unfold (Tirhakah's threat forcing a withdrawal), the enemy doubles down with a written campaign of intimidation. The chapter is teaching us that the pressure on our trust often intensifies precisely when deliverance is near — the last assault is frequently the fiercest. The chapter is also teaching us the persistent theological error of the proud: Sennacherib genuinely cannot conceive that the God of little Judah is categorically different from the dead idols of Hamath and Arpad. His whole worldview is that gods are local, tribal, and as conquerable as the nations they represent. The chapter is teaching us that this is the fatal blindness of human pride — to measure the living God by the same yardstick as the works of men's hands, never imagining that the Maker of heaven and earth (as Hezekiah's prayer will declare) is in a different category altogether. The written threat, like the spoken one, will become the very document Hezekiah spreads before the LORD — the enemy's escalating blasphemy only deepens the ground of his appeal.

And Hezekiah received the letter of the hand of the messengers, and read it: and Hezekiah went up into the house of the LORD, and spread it before the LORD. And Hezekiah prayed before the LORD, and said, O LORD God of Israel, which dwellest between the cherubims, thou art the God, even thou alone, of all the kingdoms of the earth; thou hast made heaven and earth. LORD, bow down thine ear, and hear: open, LORD, thine eyes, and see: and hear the words of Sennacherib, which hath sent him to reproach the living God. Guidance: Hezekiah spreads the letter before the LORD. This is one of the most beautiful pictures of prayer in all of Scripture. Hezekiah takes the threatening letter, goes up to the temple, and literally 'spread it before the LORD' — laying the document open before God as if to say, 'Here, LORD; read what he has written against You.' Then he prays. His prayer is a model of how to bring a crisis to God. (1) He begins with WHO God is: 'O LORD God of Israel, which dwellest between the cherubims [enthroned above the ark], thou art the God, even thou alone, of all the kingdoms of the earth; thou hast made heaven and earth.' He directly refutes Sennacherib's theology — the LORD is not one local god among many but the ONE God of ALL kingdoms, the Maker of heaven and earth. (2) He asks God to attend: 'bow down thine ear, and hear: open, LORD, thine eyes, and see.' (3) He names the real offense: Sennacherib 'hath sent him to reproach the living God.' The chapter is showing us prayer at its finest — not panicked begging but worshipful, theologically grounded appeal. Hezekiah does not start with his problem; he starts with God's greatness, and only then brings the threat. The chapter is teaching us to do exactly what Hezekiah did: take the very thing that threatens us — the letter, the diagnosis, the bad news — and spread it open before the LORD. The act is profoundly faith-filled: it acknowledges the threat fully (he read it, he understood it) while placing it entirely in God's hands. The chapter is teaching us that the foundation of bold prayer is right theology: because the LORD is 'the God, even thou alone... thou hast made heaven and earth,' Hezekiah can pray with confidence that the boasting of one earthly king is nothing before the Maker of all. The chapter is teaching us that when we face what seems unanswerable, the path forward is to spread it before the LORD, to remind ourselves (in His presence) of who He is, and to make His honor the ground of our plea.

Of a truth, LORD, the kings of Assyria have destroyed the nations and their lands, And have cast their gods into the fire: for they were no gods, but the work of men's hands, wood and stone: therefore they have destroyed them. Now therefore, O LORD our God, I beseech thee, save thou us out of his hand, that all the kingdoms of the earth may know that thou art the LORD God, even thou only. Guidance: The heart of the prayer — for God's glory among all nations. Hezekiah concedes what is true in Sennacherib's boast and then turns it into the very reason for deliverance. 'Of a truth, LORD, the kings of Assyria have destroyed the nations and their lands, And have cast their gods into the fire' — yes, Assyria really did burn the gods of the nations. But here is the decisive distinction: 'for they were no gods, but the work of men's hands, wood and stone: therefore they have destroyed them.' The reason those gods could not deliver is that they were not gods at all — just carved wood and stone. The LORD is in a wholly different category: He is the living God who made heaven and earth. Then the request, and notice its motive: 'save thou us out of his hand, THAT ALL THE KINGDOMS OF THE EARTH MAY KNOW THAT THOU ART THE LORD GOD, EVEN THOU ONLY.' Hezekiah asks for deliverance not merely so Judah will survive but so that the whole world will know the LORD is the one true God. The chapter is showing us the highest motive in prayer — the glory of God. Hezekiah does not pray 'save us because we deserve it' or even merely 'save us because we are desperate'; he prays 'save us so that all the earth may know You alone are God.' He aligns his deepest need with God's deepest purpose. The chapter is teaching us the secret of powerful prayer: to ask for what most magnifies God's name. The Rabshakeh had said no god could deliver from Assyria; Hezekiah asks the LORD to deliver precisely so that this lie will be exposed and the LORD's unique deity proclaimed to every nation. The chapter is teaching us that our prayers are most aligned with heaven when our requests are bound up with God's glory — when we long not only to be rescued but for the rescue to reveal who God truly is. The chapter is teaching us, in this model prayer, to plead God's honor and the world's knowledge of Him as the ground of our boldest asking: 'that all the kingdoms of the earth may know that thou art the LORD God, even thou only.'

Then Isaiah the son of Amoz sent to Hezekiah, saying, Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, That which thou hast prayed to me against Sennacherib king of Assyria I have heard. This is the word that the LORD hath spoken concerning him; The virgin the daughter of Zion hath despised thee, and laughed thee to scorn; the daughter of Jerusalem hath shaken her head at thee. Guidance: God answers — and turns the mockery back. Isaiah sends word: 'That which thou hast prayed to me against Sennacherib king of Assyria I have heard.' The prayer was heard the moment it was prayed. Then comes the LORD's magnificent poetic oracle against Sennacherib, beginning with a stunning reversal of the mockery. The Rabshakeh had taunted Jerusalem; now the LORD says it is Jerusalem who laughs at Sennacherib: 'The virgin the daughter of Zion hath despised thee, and laughed thee to scorn; the daughter of Jerusalem hath shaken her head at thee.' Jerusalem is called 'the virgin daughter of Zion' — the image of a city the great king assumed he would violate and conquer, but who stands untouched and now mocks her would-be ravisher. The chapter is showing us the LORD's perspective on the proud aggressor. From the human vantage point, mighty Assyria towered over tiny Judah; from heaven's vantage point, the situation is reversed — the little besieged city laughs at the boasting empire, because she has the living God on her side. The chapter is teaching us that prayer changes the true balance of a situation, even when the visible circumstances have not yet changed. The army still surrounds Judah, but the LORD has already heard, already decreed, already turned the scorn back on the scorner. The chapter is teaching us to see our intimidating circumstances from God's vantage point: what seems overwhelming is, before the Maker of heaven and earth, an object of derision. The proud blasphemer who shook his fist at the living God is now the one at whom the daughter of Zion shakes her head. The chapter is teaching us that those who mock God and threaten His people are, in the deepest reality, the ones in the absurd and doomed position — and that the LORD hears the prayer of His trusting people the moment it rises.

Whom hast thou reproached and blasphemed? and against whom hast thou exalted thy voice, and lifted up thine eyes on high? even against the Holy One of Israel. By thy messengers thou hast reproached the Lord, and hast said, With the multitude of my chariots I am come up to the height of the mountains, to the sides of Lebanon, and will cut down the tall cedar trees thereof, and the choice fir trees thereof: and I will enter into the lodgings of his borders, and into the forest of his Carmel. I have digged and drunk strange waters, and with the sole of my feet have I dried up all the rivers of besieged places. Guidance: The LORD exposes Sennacherib's pride. The oracle now confronts the king directly: 'Whom hast thou reproached and blasphemed? and against whom hast thou exalted thy voice, and lifted up thine eyes on high? even against the Holy One of Israel.' The question is devastating: Sennacherib thought he was threatening a minor vassal-state; in reality he raised his voice against 'the Holy One of Israel' — the living God. The LORD then quotes Sennacherib's own boasts back to him: 'With the multitude of my chariots I am come up to the height of the mountains... and will cut down the tall cedar trees... I have digged and drunk strange waters, and with the sole of my feet have I dried up all the rivers.' These are the typical grandiose self-glorifications of an Assyrian monarch — conquering the highest peaks, felling the mightiest forests, drying up rivers under his marching armies, as if no obstacle in heaven or earth could stand before him. The chapter is showing us the anatomy of human pride at its peak: the conqueror who credits all his victories to his own chariots, his own feet, his own power, and who imagines himself unstoppable. The chapter is teaching us that the deepest sin of the proud is not merely arrogance toward other people but the assault on God's unique holiness — to exalt oneself 'on high' is to challenge the One who alone is high. Sennacherib's boasts ('I am come up... I will cut down... I have dried up') are the language of a man who has made himself god. The chapter is teaching us that such self-exaltation, however impressive its track record, has fatally misjudged reality — the proud man has 'reproached the Holy One of Israel' without even realizing whom he was challenging. The chapter is preparing the hammer-blow of the next verses: this man who thought his power was his own is about to be told that he was never anything but a tool in the hand of the God he blasphemed.

Hast thou not heard long ago how I have done it, and of ancient times that I have formed it? now have I brought it to pass, that thou shouldest be to lay waste fenced cities into ruinous heaps. Therefore their inhabitants were of small power, they were dismayed and confounded; they were as the grass of the field, and as the green herb, as the grass on the housetops, and as corn blasted before it be grown up. But I know thy abode, and thy going out, and thy coming in, and thy rage against me. Because thy rage against me and thy tumult is come up into mine ears, therefore I will put my hook in thy nose, and my bridle in thy lips, and I will turn thee back by the way by which thou camest. Guidance: The hook in the nose — Assyria was only God's tool. Now comes the crushing reversal of Sennacherib's whole self-understanding. 'Hast thou not heard long ago how I have done it, and of ancient times that I have formed it? now have I brought it to pass.' The conquests Sennacherib boasted of as HIS achievements were in fact the LORD's doing — planned 'of ancient times' and now brought to pass by God. Sennacherib was never the true author of his victories; he was the LORD's instrument for laying waste cities whose fall God had ordained (the same truth Isaiah 10 declares: Assyria is 'the rod of mine anger'). His victims 'were as the grass... as corn blasted before it be grown up' — they fell easily because GOD had appointed it, not because Sennacherib was invincible. Then the LORD asserts total knowledge and control: 'I know thy abode, and thy going out, and thy coming in, and thy rage against me.' And the unforgettable image of judgment: 'I will put my hook in thy nose, and my bridle in thy lips, and I will turn thee back by the way by which thou camest.' This is the very image Assyrian art used for its OWN treatment of captives — leading prisoners by hooks through the nose. The LORD will do to the great conqueror exactly what the conqueror did to his captives: lead him home like a beast on a leash. The chapter is showing us the deepest humiliation of human pride: Sennacherib thought he was the subject of his own story, the mighty actor; in truth he was a tool, and now a leashed animal, in the hand of God. The chapter is teaching us a foundational truth about world history: the empires and conquerors who imagine they are the authors of events are in fact instruments in the hand of the LORD, who 'long ago' formed His purposes and now brings them to pass. The chapter is teaching us that even the most terrifying earthly power has no autonomy before God — He knows its every movement, He ordained its rise, and He can turn it back with a hook in the nose whenever He pleases. The proud man's fundamental error was thinking his power was his own; the truth is that he never had any power except what was lent him, and the moment he turned it against the Lender in blasphemy, the leash was about to be pulled.

And this shall be a sign unto thee, Ye shall eat this year such things as grow of themselves, and in the second year that which springeth of the same; and in the third year sow ye, and reap, and plant vineyards, and eat the fruits thereof. And the remnant that is escaped of the house of Judah shall yet again take root downward, and bear fruit upward. For out of Jerusalem shall go forth a remnant, and they that escape out of mount Zion: the zeal of the LORD of hosts shall do this. Guidance: The sign and the promise of a fruitful remnant. The LORD now turns from the doom of Sennacherib to comfort for Hezekiah, giving him a sign rooted in agriculture. The land had been ravaged by the Assyrian invasion, so for the first year and second year the people would eat only what grew of itself (volunteer crops from spilled grain, since normal planting had been disrupted by war); but 'in the third year sow ye, and reap, and plant vineyards, and eat the fruits thereof' — normal life would fully resume. The recovery itself would be the sign that the LORD had delivered them. Then a beautiful image of restoration: 'the remnant that is escaped of the house of Judah shall yet again take root downward, and bear fruit upward.' Judah, like a plant cut back by the invasion, would sink fresh roots and grow fruitful again. The reason: 'out of Jerusalem shall go forth a remnant... the zeal of the LORD of hosts shall do this.' The chapter is showing us that the LORD's deliverance is not merely rescue from danger but restoration to flourishing. He does not only turn back the destroyer; He promises His people will take root and bear fruit again. The chapter is teaching us the precious 'remnant' theme that runs through the prophets: even after devastation, the LORD preserves a surviving remnant through whom His purposes continue — a root from which new fruit will grow. The chapter is teaching us that recovery from a season of ruin is itself a sign of God's faithfulness: the ordinary return of sowing and reaping after a time of eating only what grows wild is the visible token that the LORD has saved His people. And the final phrase is the bedrock of all the comfort: 'the zeal of the LORD of hosts shall do this.' The deliverance and the restoration are not driven by Judah's strength or merit but by the LORD's own zealous, jealous commitment to His people and His name. The chapter is teaching us that behind every promise of restoration stands the zeal of God — His passionate determination to keep His word and glorify His name — which is the surest possible guarantee that the promise will be kept.

Therefore thus saith the LORD concerning the king of Assyria, He shall not come into this city, nor shoot an arrow there, nor come before it with shield, nor cast a bank against it. By the way that he came, by the same shall he return, and shall not come into this city, saith the LORD. For I will defend this city, to save it, for mine own sake, and for my servant David's sake. Guidance: The verdict — he shall not come into this city. The LORD's promise about Sennacherib could not be more precise or emphatic: 'He shall not come into this city, nor shoot an arrow there, nor come before it with shield, nor cast a bank against it.' Four denials — no entry, no arrow, no shield brought near, no siege-ramp raised. The Assyrian who had taken 46 fortified cities will not so much as loose a single arrow at Jerusalem. 'By the way that he came, by the same shall he return' — he will go home the way he came, accomplishing nothing against the city he was sent to take. And the ground of the whole deliverance: 'For I will defend this city, to save it, for mine own sake, and for my servant David's sake.' The chapter is showing us the two great reasons the LORD acts: His OWN name (the honor that the blaspheming Sennacherib had assailed) and His covenant with DAVID (the promise of an enduring throne and city). Note that He does NOT say 'for Hezekiah's sake' or 'for Judah's righteousness' — the city is spared for God's own glory and for the sake of the covenant promise, not because the people earned it. The chapter is teaching us that our deliverances rest ultimately on God's commitment to His own name and His own promises, not on our merit. Jerusalem is saved because the LORD will not let His name be blasphemed and will not break His covenant with David — and these are far surer foundations than any human worthiness. The chapter is teaching us the immense comfort of being included in God's covenant purposes: Judah's safety hung not on its strength against Assyria but on God's determination to glorify Himself and keep faith with David, the ancestor of the coming Messiah. The chapter is teaching us that when God defends His people 'for His own sake,' the defense is absolutely certain — for God's name and God's promises cannot fail.

And it came to pass that night, that the angel of the LORD went out, and smote in the camp of the Assyrians an hundred fourscore and five thousand: and when they arose early in the morning, behold, they were all dead corpses. So Sennacherib king of Assyria departed, and went and returned, and dwelt at Nineveh. And it came to pass, as he was worshipping in the house of Nisroch his god, that Adrammelech and Sharezer his sons smote him with the sword: and they escaped into the land of Armenia. And Esarhaddon his son reigned in his stead. Guidance: The deliverance — and every word fulfilled. The LORD acts, and the deliverance is total. 'That night, the angel of the LORD went out, and smote in the camp of the Assyrians an hundred fourscore and five thousand [185,000]: and when they arose early in the morning, behold, they were all dead corpses.' In a single night, without Judah lifting a sword, the LORD's angel destroyed the army that had terrorized the whole region. The survivors woke to a camp of the dead. The blaspheming empire that asked 'who among all the gods has delivered... that the LORD should deliver Jerusalem?' received its answer: the living God needed only one night and one angel. Then the prophecy of v7 is fulfilled clause by clause: Sennacherib 'departed, and went and returned, and dwelt at Nineveh' (he returned to his own land); and there, 'as he was worshipping in the house of Nisroch his god, that Adrammelech and Sharezer his sons smote him with the sword' (he fell by the sword in his own land — and, with bitter irony, while bowing before his powerless idol, the very thing he had trusted instead of the living God). Esarhaddon succeeded him. The chapter is showing us the complete vindication of God and of faith. The Rabshakeh's mockery is answered; Hezekiah's prayer is granted; Isaiah's every word comes to pass; the city is spared without a single arrow; and the proud blasphemer dies before his impotent god by the hands of his own sons. The chapter is teaching us the supreme lesson of these two chapters: the living God is not one deity among many but the LORD of all, and those who trust Him are delivered while those who blaspheme Him are destroyed. The chapter is teaching us that no power on earth, however overwhelming, can stand against the LORD when He rises to defend His name and His people — 185,000 of the mightiest army on earth fell silently in the dark, and the great king crept home to die. The chapter is teaching us that the deepest reality is always the one the eye of faith sees: from the wall, Judah seemed doomed; in truth, the doomed one was Assyria. And the chapter is teaching us the irony of misplaced trust carried to its end — Sennacherib, who scorned trust in the LORD and trusted his own might and his god Nisroch, was cut down in the very temple of the idol that could not save him. The chapter stands forever as the great Old Testament proof that the LORD alone is God, that He hears the prayer of the trusting, and that He will not share His glory with another nor leave His name reproached.
